topic: Theme 3: Communication and the World Around Us (Travel and Tourism; Media and Technology; The Environment and Where People Live)
subject: spanish

items.q: What is the Spanish word for 'mobile phone' (in Spain)?
items.misconception: El celular (which is used in Latin America).
items.why: Vocabulary varies by region. Spain: móvil, ordenador (computer), coche (car). Latin America: celular, computadora, carro/auto. Students mix these. Examiners accept both, but consistency matters. Móvil is most common in GCSE/A-level.

items.q: How do you say 'I'm going to the beach' (talking to someone elsewhere) in Spanish?
items.why: Students confuse ir (to go) and venir (to come). IR = movement AWAY from speaker; VENIR = movement TOWARDS speaker. 'I'm going there' = voy; 'I'm coming here' = vengo. Critical for holidays/travel vocab.

items.q: How do you say 'I am going to visit Spain' in Spanish?
items.why: Students drop the 'a' or use a conjugated verb. The near future is IR (conjugated) + A + INFINITIVE. The 'a' is obligatory: voy A comer, vamos A ver, vas A estudiar. Heavily tested for holiday/future plans.

items.q: Which sentence means 'I never use Twitter'?
items.why: It's Nunca uso Twitter.. “Nunca yo uso Twitter.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: to download. “to delete” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many subject-verb endings does Spanish typically have per tense (one per person)?
items.why: The real figure is 6 endings. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
